Summary: Code replacement library example models result in build error if no compiler is installed
Description: If you have not installed and registered a compiler with MATLAB, the following error occurs when you build an code replacement library example model: CrlTableName  cannot be found in registry In the message, CrlTableName  is the name of the code replacement library configured for the example model being built.
Workaround: Install a supported compiler.Register the compiler with MATLAB.Rebuild the example model.

Summary: bdroot is incorrect after sltest.harness.load
Description: After loading a test harness using the function sltest.harness.load , bdroot  points to the main model when the function should point to the loaded test harness.
Workaround: Programmatically select a block in the Test Harness model using set_param(blkHandle, 'Selected', 'on') to ensure bdroot is set correctly.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Possible incorrect answer from concatenation  of variable-size matrices
Description: MATLAB?  does not allow concatenation if the component matrices have different sizes for a dimension that is not concatenated. For example, [ones(3,3), ones(4,4)] is not allowed because the number of rows is different. In some cases, the code generation software does not detect the dimension size mismatch and generates incorrect code. The code generation software fails to detect the dimension size mismatch when for all component arrays, the first few dimensions are known at compile time, but the subsequent dimensions are variable size. For example, [ones(1,1,N), ones(1,1)] . If a concatenation causes this issue, the concatenation fails in MATLAB. This issue occurs for code generation using MATLAB?  Coder?. It also occurs for code in a MATLAB Function block or in a MATLAB System block. ? ?
Workaround: Verify that the code that includes the concatenation runs correctly in MATLAB using the same input data.If you want code generation to produces an error for a dimension size mismatch, use cat to concatenate the arrays. For example, replaceb = zeros(1, n);c = 1;a = [b; c];withb = zeros(1, n);c = 1; a = cat(1, b, c);??

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Literal character escape sequence might be incorrect?in generated code
Description: In rare circumstances, the generated code might contain an incorrect literal character escape sequence. For example the generated code might contain an unterminated character such as the following code: myModel_fcnCall('  instead of the correct escape sequence '\x00' . The correct code is as follows: myModel_fcnCall('x00');?  This issue might occur for a modeling pattern consisting of all of the following elements?: A MATLAB Function block? The use of character constants corresponding to a literal escape sequence?such as ?'char(0) ' in the MATLAB Function block? The character constant is passed as an argument to a function call

Summary: Unable to download project from the Polyspace Metrics server
Description: When you try to download results with many checks from the Polyspace Metrics server you can see two issues: The download never completes. You can wait 10-30 minutes, but it never starts. The Polyspace Metrics server process uses 100% cpu time for one cpu core and it does not respond to other clients anymore until a timeout occurs or you restart the Polyspace Metrics server.
Workaround: Try this suggestion:Set Quality Objectives to ON on Polyspace Metrics web page before downloading results.Click on a link where tooltip highlights a few findings.After the download has been successful, change the filter in Polyspace User Interface from 'Polyspace Metrics' to 'All checks'. You will be able to see all finding including the desired selection of checks.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Passing a colon as an argument to a function handle generates code instead of throwing an error
Description: In MATLAB, passing colon operator to a function handle is not allowed:? >> f = @(x) x+1;  >> f(:)  Input arguments to function include colon operator. To input the colon character, use ':' instead.   However, MATLAB Coder will generate code for this, instead of throwing an error.

Summary: Code generation may fail for a model containing an enumeration class that derives from the integer data type
Description: Code generation may fail for a model meeting the following conditions: The model contains a Simulink.Parameter  of the Enum  Data type . The enumeration class derives from one of the following integer data types: uint8 , int8 , int16 , or uint16 . On the Hardware Implementation  Pane, the Number of bits for the char, short, or int data types does not match the integer data type size in the model's enumeration class.
Workaround: Derive the enumeration class from an integer data type that has the same number of bits as the char, short, or int data types on the Hardware Implementation pane.

Summary: Reading the fields of some MathWorks-created objects, including Simulink.Bus objects, can be slower in R2015b
Description: Reading data from fields of certain MathWorks-created objects can be slower in R2015b than in previous releases, especially if the objects are stored in arrays or in arrays of structures.  The primary object that MathWorks is aware of that encounters this problem is the Simulink.Bus  object. A test at MathWorks showed  performance that was about 20% slower in R2015b than in R2015a. However, the performance difference varies based on the objects and machine.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Passing an expression that uses?the colon operator?to a function handle behaves differently in generated code than in MATLAB
Description: Suppose that you have an expression of the form foo(a:b)  or foo(a:b:c)  in your MATLAB code, where foo  is a function handle. If the computation of a:b  or a:b:c  uses floating point arithmetic,?the values computed for a:b ?or a:b:c  might differ between the generated code and MATLAB.  In such cases, the code generator and MATLAB use different rounding-off schemes to calculate the elements of the vector that is passed to the function handle foo . This causes a different distribution of the round-off error over the vector elements in the generated code and in MATLAB.
Workaround: Assign the expression?that uses the colon operator to a variable. Pass this variable to the?function handle. For example, rewritef = @foo;f(x:y:z);asf = @foo;a = x:y:z;f(a);

Summary: When a model is built in standalone mode, the message list in the CAN Pack/Unpack block dialog shows as empty
Description: This issue happens when the target boot mode is 'Standalone' and the model contains CAN Pack/Unpack blocks. Once the build process completes, the CAN Pack/Unpack block dialog box no longer shows the CAN messages, which were originally populated by using the specified CANdb file.
Workaround: To prevent this issue, please install the attached patch.Without the patch, the following workaround is available:Call 'clear classes' from MATLAB command window. This will cause the message list to be repopulated once the CAN Pack/Unpack block dialog is opened.Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.For each file that appears in the .zip file, find the corresponding file in your existing <MATLAB> folder and make a backup of it. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.*Notes for Windows Systems:You need administrative privileges to save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder. To save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder, open this Bug Report in a browser started in administrator mode.If you see "Error: Access is denied" when attempting to extract the .zip file, you may need to start WinZip in administrator mode. Do this by navigating to WinZip.exe or the WinZip Start menu entry, right-clicking on it, and selecting Run as administrator.From within WinZip, find and open the downloaded .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.

Summary: Inconsistent behavior with Highlight to source when 3 bus creators are used in a row
Description: When three bus creator blocks are used in a row to create a nested bus and a bus selector is used to select signals from this bus, the Highlight to source  from the bus selector block stops at the first bus creator if the selected signal is not the last signal in the bus.
Workaround: Open the bus selector's block dialog. Find the signal you want to highlight to source in the tree view and click the Find button to find the source of the signal.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated C code for Simulink function call array arguments using AUTOSAR schema version 3.x
Description: Embedded Coder generates incorrect C code for a model configured for the AUTOSAR target if both of the following conditions exist: In the model, a Simulink Function Caller block calls a Simulink function with an array argument. AUTOSAR schema version 3.x is selected for the model.  Under these conditions, the model build generates function call argument code that is appropriate for schema version 4.x, but not appropriate for 3.x. For example: real_T (*tmp)[2];...Rte_Call_cport_Fcn(rtb_callFcn, *tmp); For AUTOSAR schema version 3.x, array arguments are passed as pointer to array type rather than pointer to base type. The function call should not dereference the pointer. For example: real_T (*tmp)[2]; ... Rte_Call_cport_Fcn(rtb_callFcn, tmp); Depending on compiler warning and error settings, compilation might generate an error, or might implicitly cast from one pointer type to another and not trigger an error.

Summary: MATLAB tooltips are empty under certain Linux window managers (e.g., Unity)
Description: Under certain window managers on Linux (e.g., Unity and certain versions of GNOME), MATLAB tooltips (e.g., Editor datatips, code analyzer tooltips, Current Folder Browser tooltips) can appear as empty gray rectangles under certain Linux window managers.This is a problem with underlying Java technology, as documented here .

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Error check for dynamic memory allocation on handle class objects might not occur resulting in wrong answer
Description: MATLAB Coder might generate incorrect code if the following conditions hold:? You store handle objects in persistent variables You create new handle objects when the program is run, and these newly created objects are stored in the persistent variable repeatedly  The error check that is supposed to detect and report these unsupported cases fails and results in code that produces wrong answers. A more detailed explanation follows. MATLAB Coder currently does not support dynamic memory allocation for handle class objects. When dynamic memory allocation is required for handle class objects, MATLAB Coder must error.? Consider this example where MATLAB Coder produces an error. The function creates multiple handle class objects inside a loop. The code generator has no information on which handle object is made persistent at compile time. Therefore the code generator stores each object and uses dynamic memory allocation because the number of objects is unbounded. function y = myloop(n, j)?? for i = 1:n?  ? ? h = my_handle_object();  ? ? subfcn(i, j, h);?  end?  function y = subfcn(i, j, h)?  persistent p;?  if i == j?  ? ? p = h;?  end?  To determine whether dynamic memory allocation is needed, MATLAB Coder attempts to detect for-loops and detect where handle class objects might survive outside for-loops, such as in persistent variables, and thereby require dynamic memory allocation to be stored. However, in cases where there are no for-loops at all, MATLAB Coder can fail to recognize when dynamic memory allocation is required.? Consider an example program that computes the Fibonacci sequence, where MATLAB Coder fails to produce an error. function y = myfib  ? ? persistent p p1; ? ? ? if isempty(p)  ? ? ? ? p = MyClass; p.count = 0;  ? ? ? ? p1 = MyClass; p1.count = 1;  ? ? end  ? ? p2 = p1;  ? ? p1 = p;  ? ? p = MyClass;  ? ? p.count = p1.count + p2.count;  ? ? y = p.count;  end  classdef MyClass < handle ? ?? ? properties  ? ? ? ? count ? ? ? end  end  When executing this function in MATLAB, you get:? >> myfib  1?  >> myfib?  1?  >> myfib?  2?  >> myfib?  3?  >> myfib?  5?  >> ...?  When you compile?myfib ?to a MEX function using codegen, and execute the MEX function, you get incorrect answers:? >> codegen myfib  >> myfib_mex?  1?  >> myfib_mex?  1?  >> myfib_mex?  2?  >> myfib_mex?  4?  >> ...?  This program requires dynamic memory allocation (with garbage collection) to properly function. The program has no explicit for-loops. There is an implicit for-loop because you can call the top-level function an unknown number of times. MATLAB Coder is unable to detect this contingency and fails to issue an error.

Summary: Mapping of Simulink block to AUTOSAR elements lost if mapped block is a reference to a library block
Description: In the Configure AUTOSAR Interface dialog box, Simulink-AUTOSAR Mapping  view, you can map a Simulink Function block, Function Caller block, or Rate Transition block to corresponding AUTOSAR elements. The specified mapping is saved with the model. However, if the Simulink block that you mapped is a reference to a library block, the mapping is not correctly preserved in the model save. When you reopen the model and view Simulink-to-AUTOSAR mapping, the block is unmapped.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: In some cases, coder.ceval calls that pass arguments by reference can cause wrong answers in generated code
Description: In some cases, when one variable is assigned to another variable, and then both variables are passed by reference in a coder.ceval  call, the generated code can produce wrong answers.  For example, consider the following  MATLAB?code: x = y;z = 0;z = coder.ceval('my_fcn', coder.rref(x), coder.ref(y)); The my_fcn  call might be generated as: my_fcn(y, y); If my_fcn  writes to the second coder.ref()  input variable, a subsequent read of the first coder.rref()  input variable can have the wrong value because the same variable y  was used at the function call-site for both input arguments. For example: double my_fcn(double* x,double* y){  double z;  *y = *y - 10.0;  z = *x + 1.0;   return z;} For an example model please see 'example_issue_1228023.slx' in the attached zip file 'issue_1228023.zip'. This issue can occurwhen generating code for Simulink models with MATLAB Function blocks or MATLABFunctions in Stateflow charts that contain these kinds of coder.ceval  calls.

Summary: Rapid double-clicking in Simulink Editor can crash MATLAB
Description: Rapid interactions with the Simulink Editor, i.e., rapid double-clicking followed by other mouse events, sometimes trigger assertions that crash MATLAB. The text "Assertion failed: Deviant 'root deviant' : object of type 'SLM3I.Annotation' [id = -1 ] is not present at kernel/deviant/Deviant.cpp" appears in the crash log.

Summary: MATLAB might crash while debugging with the Workspace browser open
Description: MATLAB might crash during a debugging session using the dbstop  function when the Workspace browser is open.  This issue is more frequently encountered while using the following: dbstop if error
Workaround: Close the Workspace browser while trying to debug your code.  You can reopen the browser after finishing your debugging session by executing the workspace command.

Summary: Opening a block diagram test harness throws a warning if the name of the referenced Simulink model in the test harness is changed on disk
Description: When you open a test harness associated with a top-level Simulink model block diagram, if the name of the Simulink model referenced in the test harness has changed on disk, a warning similar to the following is thrown: The model 'mymodel' referenced by 'mymodel_Harness1/mymodel' is not found.  The warning occurs because the model name in the Model block is pointing to the old model name. ?
Workaround: Open the Test Harness.Update the model name in the Model Reference block to point to the updated model name. Re-save the model.

Summary: Invalid results for models containing Stateflow charts with State Machine Type set to Moore
Description: If you have a model containing a Stateflow Moore chart with inputs that are not directly connected to root-level inports, and these inputs cause state change in the chart, analyzing the model using Simulink Design Verifier can result in: Generated test cases that fail to satisfy their intended objectives Property proving counterexamples that fail to violate their intended objectives

Summary: MATLAB might execute a stale cached version of a code file overwritten using fopen and fclose
Description: If you run a MATLAB program that overwrites a code file using fopen  and fclose  and then runs it, MATLAB might incorrectly execute a stale cached version of the code file.
Workaround: After calling fclose and before running the overwritten file, insert the command clear myFilename.

Summary: Orbisnap library fails to open on Red Hat and SUSE Enterprise Linux platforms?
Description: You cannot open the Simulink 3D Animation library for the Orbisnap viewer on Red Hat or SUSE Enterprise Linux platforms.
Workaround: In the installation files for Simulink 3D Animation, in the toolbox/sl3d/orbisnap/bin/glnxa64/ folder, rename the libopenal.so file to libopenal.so.1 (in other words, add a .1 extension).

Summary: Assertion failure when calling MATLAB functions from Stateflow with a case-insensitive match
Description: Simulink might throw an assertion error when you update or run a model with a Stateflow chart that contains a call to a function with a case-insensitive match such as Run  instead of run  or Help  instead of help . Only Stateflow charts that use MATLAB action language are affected. The error message begins with: b:\matlab\toolbox\stateflow\src\stateflow\cdr\cdr_eml_construct.cpp at line 374:emlData != NULL

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Switch or Multiport Switch block in a multi-instance reusable subsystem can generate wrong code
Description: Your Simulink model can generate wrong code on building the model, if it meets all the following criteria: Your model has two or more identical atomic subsystems having a Switch or Multiport Switch block inside the subsystem. The control port of these blocks are driven by Constant blocks outside the subsystem. The values of the Constant block are such that they allow the Switch or Multiport Switch  block to select one input in one atomic subsystem and another input in another atomic subsystem.  On building this model, the generated code for the block does not have a conditional statement to handle the different inputs from the Constant block outside the subsystem. Instead, it is incorrectly optimized to take the input that is derived from the control port value of the instance of this subsystem that executes first. Hence, other instances may get the incorrect output from their Switch or Multiport Switch block.
Workaround: To work around this problem, clear the Inline Parameters check box in Model Configuration Parameters->Optimization->Signals and Parameters->Simulation and Code Generation and rebuild your model.Alternatively, you can add a Signal Conversion block after the Constant block.

Summary: Exporting a Simulink model that contains test harnesses to a previous Simulink version in MDL format silently discards harnesses and harness information
Description: If you export a Simulink model that contains one or more test harnesses to a previous version of Simulink in MDL format, the exported model generated by the export process does not contain the test harnesses or test harness information.  However, no warning is thrown to indicate that harnesses and harness information will not be saved. This occurs because test harnesses in Simulink Test are only supported in SLX format for R2015a (Simulink Test version 1.0). If you export a Simulink model that contains one or more test harnesses specifically to R2014b in SLX format, harnesses and harness information are not deleted even though it will not be possible to work with the harnesses in R2014b.

Summary: Compile time error when assigning to structure type variables
Description: Simulink Coder might generate uncompilable C/C++ code for a model that contains Simulink.Bus  objects with identical fields but different names in conjunction with structure parameters. The C/C++ code compile time error may contain wording similar to the following:  error: incompatible types when assigning to type ˇ®BusType1ˇŻ from type ˇ®BusType2ˇŻ"
Workaround: Avoid using structurally identical bus objects with different names in a model that uses MATLAB structures along with those bus types.

Summary: Cannot simultaneously add multiple channels, from certain NI DSA devices, to a session
Description: When you add multiple channels from certain NI Dynamic Signal Acquisition (DSA) devices, such as NI PXI-4461, PXI-4462, PCI-4461, or PCI-4462 to a session, you might see the following error: s = daq.createSession('ni');addAnalogInputChannel(s, 'Dev1',0:3,'Voltage');  Warning: This property must be the same for all channels on this device. All channels associated with this device were updated.   NI Error -200106: Property must have the same value for all channels on this device. Invalid Input: DAQmx_AI_EnhancedAliasRejectionEnableTask Name: _unnamedTask<9F>Status Code: -200106
Workaround: Add channels one at a time using a for loop:nChannels = 4;for i = 0:nChannels-1 addAnalogInputChannel(s, 'Dev1', i, 'Voltage');end

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Simulation answer in classic initialization mode is sensitive to subsystem block names in certain modeling patterns involving Merge blocks
Description: When using classic initialization mode, i.e., when the option Configuration Parameters > Diagnostics > Data Validity > Underspecified initialization detection  is set to Classic , the simulation answer is sensitive to subsystem block naming but code generation answer is not. Therefore, there can be a mismatch between the simulation and the code generation answers. Your model can be susceptible to this issue if it has one  of the following patterns: Basic Pattern: A model has atomic subsystems A  and B  that are connected to the Merge block with an empty inital condition (IC) (i.e., [] ) Atomic subystems A  and B  contain conditionally-executed subsystems E1  and E2  with two different ICs directly feeding the Outports of  A  and B , respectively.  In this case, the simulation answer could be sensitive to the alphabetical ordering between the subsystem names A  and B (for example, the answer might change if the subsystem A  is renamed to C ). Here are other cases where this problem is visible as well: A variation of the basic pattern in which A  or B  are replaced by conditionally-executed subsystems with empty ICs (i.e., [] ) . A variation of the basic pattern, in which E1  or E2  are be replaced by conditionally-executed Stateflow charts with non-empty ICs. A variation of the basic pattern in which the Merge block is absorbed inside subsystem A , and thus is connected to the Outport of subsystem B  directly via the Inport of subsystem A . In this case the Merge block IC does not have to be empty.
Workaround: To avoid this problem, upgrade your model to simplified initialization mode.To do this, use the Model Advisor check titled Check consistency of initialization parameters for Outport and Merge blocks. You can also manually set Configuration Parameters > Diagnostics > Data Validity > Underspecified initialization detection parameter to Simplified. However, the use of Model Advisor is recommended.You can also avoid this problem for the basic patterns and variations #1 & #2 by setting a non-empty IC on the Merge block in classic initialization mode. Pattern #3 exhibits an incorrect use of the Merge block. Merging of outputs of conditionally executed subsystems should not be done across the input-side of non-virtual subsystem boundaries.

Summary: MATLAB? might crash when changing display configurations
Description: MATLAB might crash if you change the display configuration while MATLAB is running on a Windows? or Macintosh system. For example, disconnecting a display on a dual-display system while MATLAB is running is one scenario that can cause the crash. The crash occurs when you execute MATLAB commands after the configuration change. The resulting stack trace might contain the following reference: gbtutils::MixedModeManager::operator=
Workaround: Perform either of the following to avoid the crash:Execute the command, get(groot), before changing the display configuration.Restart MATLAB after changing the display configuration.

Summary: Code generation might fail for a model containing a nonvirtual bus element
Description: Code generation might fail for a model that contains a nonvirtual bus signal in which one of elements connects to either a Constant block or Ground block or to no block. When code generation fails, you will receive an error message similar to the following:?  Code generation assertion 'varInfo != NULL' failed in 'rtwcg_utils/rtwcg_tlc.cpp:384'.

Summary: Calling which can cause MATLAB to crash in certain situations.
Description: A bug in the which  command can cause MATLAB to crash. The crash can occur after a change to the MATLAB path or a call to clear  or clear all . The stack traces in the crash-dumps often show calls to the which  function.
Workaround: There is no reliable workaround. In some situations, you can avoid the crash by not calling which immediately after path changes or calls to the clear function.

Summary: Quality and performance issues with plot markers
Description: Markers in plots exhibit a number of quality and performance issues: Displaying a large number of markers causes a significant slowdown in graphics display and pan/zoom/rotate frame rate>> plot(rand(1e6,1), 'x')  Circle-shaped markers are drawn as octagons>> plot(rand(100,1), 'o')  Markers on wide lines change shape>> plot(rand(100,1), 'o', 'MarkerSize', 15, 'LineWidth', 10)
Workaround: Use smaller point markers to improve performance when displaying large numbers of them>> plot(rand(1e6,1),'.') Use fewer markers for a clearer display >> plot(rand(1e4,1),'x')Plot the markers using smaller line widths>> plot(rand(100,1),'MarkerSize', 15, 'LineWidth', 2)

Summary: Error may occur with Java classes when multiple deployed libraries are loaded
Description: An error with undefined Java class may occur with deployed library components (e.g., C/C++ libraries, .NET assemblies, Excel add-ins, Java packages, MPS archives), even if the JAR files containing those classes are added to the Java dynamic path using the javaaddpath  command. Deployed standalone executables are not affected. Below is an example error, but other errors related to other classes can occur. No constructor 'com.bloomberglp.blpapi.Session' with matching signature found The bug can manifest under the following conditions: The MATLAB code being deployed depends on a custom or third party Java class, such that the deployed component depends on a JAR file containing that class The component being deployed is a library Multiple such deployed libraries are loaded into the driver application  Under the above conditions, the first loaded library will work fine, but subsequent libraries will fail to find the Java class packaged in the JAR file.
Workaround: Add the JAR file path to the Java static class path of the deployed component.  To do this:In MATLAB, run the prefdir command to obtain the MATLAB preferences directory, e.g., C:\Users\username\AppData\Roaming\MathWorks\MATLAB\R2014aIn the MATLAB preferences directory, create a text file called javaclasspath.txtIn the javaclasspath.txt file, add the JAR file path on its own line.  This path should be the path where the JAR file is expected to be found on the target machine after deployment, e.g., C:\blp\api\APIv3\JavaAPI\lib\blpapi3.jarRedeploy the component

Summary: MATLAB crashes on Linux with the error 'dlopen: cannot load any more object with static TLS'
Description: MATLAB can crash on Linux systems with NVIDIA supported hardware OpenGL. The crash results in an error message containing this text: dlopen: cannot load any more object with static TLS This issue typically occurs during startup. However, it also can occur within an established MATLAB session when using Simulink, MuPAD, parallel computing, or graphics-related commands.
Workaround: These are two workarounds available:  Use software OpenGL by starting MATLAB from the Linux command line using:matlab -softwareopengl  Force the NVIDIA OpenGL library to load early by starting MATLAB from the Linux command line using:matlab -r "opengl info"

Summary: inv shows precision degradation when operating on ill-conditioned real symmetric matrices.
Description: For ill-conditioned real symmetric matrices, inv  might produce a less accurate result in releases R2013a through R2015b when compared to past releases. For example,   >> A = hilb(8);  >> X = inv(A);  >> leftRes = norm(X*A-eye(size(A)))./(norm(X)*norm(A)) leftRes = ???2.3209e-11   The left normwise residual leftRes  is expected to be at machine precision, which is roughly 1e-16  for IEEE double. You can see that leftRes  is around 1e-11 , which is too large.
Workaround: You can avoid this problem with the following code substitutions:Replace inv(A) with A\eye(size(A),class(A)). Replace inv(A)*B with A\B.

Summary: Sporadic "No matching files were found" error during parallel code generation
Description: When building a model for which referenced models are built in parallel, code generation might sporadically fail with an error message that includes the following line: No matching files were found.
Workaround: You can visit Bug Report ID 1336928 to download a patch that addresses this issue.

Summary: On Linux, models containing a Simscape Power Systems block can crash MATLAB when simulated with Default parameter behavior parameter set to Inlined
Description: Simscape Power Systems models can crash MATLAB on Linux platform when simulated with the Configuration Parameters > Optimization > Signals and Parameters > Default parameter behavior  parameter set to Inlined  . Here is a snippet of the stack trace associated with the crash: Stack Trace (from fault):[  0] 0x00007f9e648740e8 SLCompBlockPlatformInfo::getCompRemovedViaBlkReduction() const at /local/R2015a/src/simulink/../../derived/glnxa64/src/include/sl_engine_classes/platform/../../../../../../src/sl_engine_classes/export/include/sl_engine_classes/platform/SLCompBlockPlatformInfo.hpp:680 (in /local/R2015a/bin/glnxa64/libmwsimulink.so)[  1] 0x00007f9e65312c1b WriteModelParameters(RTWInfo*) at /local/R2015a/src/simulink/sl_engin/rtwgen_parameter.cpp:1991 (in /local/R2015a/bin/glnxa64/libmwsimulink.so)

Summary: plotyy charts do not zoom correctly when selecting reset to original view option
Description: Charts containing two Y-Axes, including those created with the plotyy  and plotbins  functions, might zoom incorrectly when using the reset to original view  context menu option.
Workaround: Instead, use either the Zoom In or Zoom Out tool and click on the chart until the original view is restored.

Summary: MATLAB terminates with an Illegal Instruction error when run on a machine with AMD Jaguar processors.
Description: On machines with AMD processors from the Jaguar family, MATLAB terminates with an Illegal Instruction error when computing certain linear algebra operations.
Workaround: For MATLAB releases R2013b and R2014a, you can work around this problem by setting two environment variables prior to starting MATLAB. Set MATLAB_DISABLE_CBWR to 1 and set MKL_CBWR to SSE4_2 by referring to the following MATLAB Answers article:https://www.mathworks.com/matlabcentral/answers/223482-how-do-i-set-add-or-change-environment-variablesFor MATLAB releases R2014b and later, please contact Technical Support for further help via the following link:https://www.mathworks.com/support/contact_us

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect initializaion in classic initialization mode for function-call subsystems with a Merge block feeding its Outport in certain conditions
Description: There may be incorrect initialization for simulation and generated code for models that satisfy the following conditions: The model has the option Configuration Parameters > Diagnostics > Data Validity > Underspecified initialization detection  set to Classic.  The model has a function-call subsystem that has a Merge block with an explicitly-set initial condition (i.e., Initial Output  not set to [] ) feeding the subsystem Outport with a different initial condition (i.e., Initial Output  set to a value other than []  or the Merge block's Initial Output ) The function-call initator (modeled using, e.g., a function-call generator block or a Stateflow chart outputting an event to Simulink) responsible for running the above subsystem is placed inside a different conditionally executed subsystem.  For example, consider a model with an enabled subsystem and a function-call subsystem. The function-call subsystem contains a Merge block (Initial Output  = 3) that is connected to an Outport (Initial Output  = 4) of the function-call subsystem. The enabled subsystem contains a function-call generator that is connected to an Outport of the enabled subsystem. This Outport is connected to the function-call trigger port of the function-call subsystem. Simulation and generated code may produce answer 3 in classic initialization mode.
Workaround: You can avoid this problem by doing one of the following.Upgrade your model to simplified initialization mode. To do this, use the Model Advisor check titled Check consistency of initialization parameters for Outport and Merge blocks. You can also manually set Configuration Parameters > Diagnostics > Data Validity > Underspecified initialization detection parameter to Simplified to upgrade the model to simplified initialization mode. However, the use of Model Advisor is recommended to comprehensively identify all the issues that you need to resolve in your model before turning on simplified initialization mode.Set Initial Output of the function-call subsystem Outport block to [] or to same value as that of the driving Merge block.Insert a Signal Conversion block with its Output set to Signal Copy between the Merge block and the function-call subsystem Outport.

Summary: Text background color is incorrect when using software OpenGL? on Linux? systems with GraphicsSmoothing disabled
Description: When using software OpenGL on a Linux system, the text object background color might be incorrect when the GraphicsSmoothing  property of the figure is set to 'off' .
Workaround: Set the GraphicsSmoothing property to 'on'.The GraphicsSmoothing property has no effect when using software OpenGL on Linux systems. Thus, it is not necessary to explicitly set GraphicsSmoothing to 'off'.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Inconsistent initialization between simulation and code generation in classic initialization mode for models containing Merge blocks inside triggered subsystems
Description: There may be inconsistency between the initial values used for signals in simulation and in code generation for models that satisfy all of these conditions: The model has the following option Configuration Parameters > Diagnostics > Data Validity > Underspecified initialization detection  set to Classic . The model has a Merge block with explicitly specified initial condition (i.e., Initial Output  set to a value different from [] ) inside a triggered subsystem. The output of the Merge block is connected to the input of one of the subsystem's Outport blocks and this block sets a different initial condition (i.e., Initial Output  not set to []  and different from the Merge block's setting for Initial Output ).  For example, consider a triggered subsystem in a model that has a Merge block (Initial Output  = 3) feeding a subsystem Outport (Initial Output  = 4). In the generated code, the subsystem initial output is 3 (Merge block), while in simulation, the subsystem initial output is 4 (Outport block).
Workaround: You can avoid this problem by doing one of the following.Upgrade your model to simplified initialization mode. To do this, use the Model Advisor check titled Check consistency of initialization parameters for Outport and Merge blocks. You can also manually set Configuration Parameters > Diagnostics > Data Validity > Underspecified initialization detection parameter to Simplified to upgrade the model to simplified initialization mode. However, the use of Model Advisor is recommended.Alternatively, insert a Signal Conversion block with its Output set to Signal Copy between the Merge block and the subsystem Outport.

Summary: Lookup Table Dynamic block outputs at fixed-in-minor-step sample time when inputs are continuous sample time
Description: When the inputs to the Lookup Table Dynamic block are continuous sample time, the output of this block are fixed-in-minor-step, but they should be continuous.
Workaround: Follow these steps to work around this problem.Use the Prelookup block in combination with the Interpolation Using PreLookup block For the Prelookup block, set Breakpoint Data Source set to From PortFor the Interpolation Using PreLookup block, set Table Data Source set to From Port.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Inconsistent initialization between simulation and generated code for certain improper usage of Merge block
Description: There is an inconsistency in the initialization in simulation and code generation for models containing the following pattern. A Merge block with explicit Initial Output specified (i.e., not set to '[] ') is placed inside a non-virtual subsystem. At least one of the inputs to the Merge block comes from a Model Reference block that is placed outside the non-virtual subsystem and feeds the Merge block via the Inport of the non-virtual subsystem. The said Model Reference block sets a different Initial Output specification (i.e., not set to '[] ' and different from the Initial Output of the Merge block). This Initial Output setting could be specified on a root-level Outport if referenced model itself is conditionally executed (i.e., has root-level control port(s) such as function call, enable etc.). Alternatively, it could be specified at the Outport of a conditionally executed subsystem that feeds directly to the root-level Outport of the referenced model.  This is an improper usage of Merge block. Merge blocks should be used to merge the outputs of conditionally-executed subsystems or conditionally-executed Model blocks. This merging should not  be done across an input-side boundary of a non-virtual subsystem via an Inport.
Workaround: To avoid this problem, do not merge outputs of conditionally executed subsystems across the input-side of non-virtual subsystem boundaries.

Summary: Class definition error when instantiating if contained within another folder with "@" symbol
Description: If a class definition is contained within a folder with an "@" inside the folder name, MATLAB throws an error when you instantiate the class. This does not apply to class folders, which start with "@". Consider the following reproduction steps. Create a folder named my@folder . Inside that folder create a class folder named @myClass . Inside the @myClass  folder, create an empty class definition in myClass.m .   classdef myClass   % empty class definition   end  Change your current folder to 'my@folder' and instantiate the class at the command prompt by typing myClass . MATLAB throws the following error:   Error using myClass   Error: File: /my@folder/@myClass/myClass.m Line: 1 Column: 10   A class definition must be in an "@" directory.

Summary: Simulink Scopes are restricted to 4 displays in one column
Description: The layout of multiple displays on a Simulink Scope is restricted to 4 rows by 4 columns. Therefore, it is not possible to stack more than 4 displays in one column.
Workaround: Install the attached patch to increase the maximum layout of multiple displays to 16 rows by 16 columns. Click and drag inside the layout tool to extend the layout beyond 4 x 4 and up to 16 x 16 displays.Installation instructionsLog into an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Start MATLABTo locate your MATLAB installation folder, in the MATLAB Command Window, type matlabroot.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <matlabroot> folder.For each file that appears in the .zip file, find the corresponding file in your existing matlabroot folder and make a backup copy. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh):unzip -l <matlabroot>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <matlabroot> folder.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh): cd <matlabroot>unzip <.zip file>Delete the .zip file.Notes for Windows Systems:You need administrative privileges to save the .zip file in the <matlabroot> folder. To save the .zip file in the <matlabroot> folder, open this Bug Report in a browser that you started in administrator mode.If you see ˇ°Error: Access is deniedˇ± when attempting to extract the .zip file, you may need to start WinZip in administrator mode. Navigate to WinZip.exe or the WinZip Start menu entry, right-click on it, and then select Run as administrator.From within WinZip, find and open the downloaded .zip file. Extract the contents of the file to your <matlabroot> folder. Check Overwrite Existing Files to replace the existing files.Note:A large number of displays may impact the performance of a Scope depending on the capabilities of your machine.Change log:October 15, 2015: attachment_788058_15b_2015-10-15.zip: initial version

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Inconsistent initialization between simulation and code generation due to improper Merge block usage
Description: Initialization of a Simulink model may be inconsistent for simulation and code generation when the following conditions are true: The model uses classic initialization mode, i.e., Model Configuration  > Diagnostics  > Data Validity  > Underspecified initialization detection  parameter is set to Classic . The model contains a Merge block driven by a Stateflow chart and a conditionally executed subsystem. The Stateflow chart initializes its output to a value different from that of the conditionally executed subsystem outport. The Initial output  parameter of the Merge block is not explicitly specified, i.e., set to an empty matrix ([] ). Neither the Stateflow chart nor the conditionally executed subsystem execute at the first time step.  ?
Workaround: Use one of the following approaches:Change the initialization mode of the model to Simplified. In simplified initialization mode, the default Initial output value ([]) of the Merge block is treated as zero. Consult Convert from Classic to Simplified Initialization Mode in the Simulink documentation to address conversion issues. Set the Initial output parameter of the Merge block, unless the output port of the Merge block connects to another Merge block. ?

Summary: AND operation not precise for variables based on mod
Description: The verification may be imprecise on AND  operations if the operands are type  variables that use the mod  attribute. Example: package test is     type Word is mod 65536;     type Byte is mod 256;     function Lsb (W : IN Word) return Byte; end test; package body test is    function Lsb (W : Word) return Byte is    begin        return Byte (W AND 16#00ff#); -- Should be a green overflow check.   end Lsb; end test;
Workaround: Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.For each file that appears in the .zip file, find the corresponding file in your existing <MATLAB> folder and make a backup of it. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh):unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh): cd <MATLAB>unzip <.zip file>Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.*Notes for Windows Systems:You need administrative privileges to save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder. To save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder, open this Bug Report in a browser started in administrator mode.If you see ˇ°Error: Access is deniedˇ± when attempting to extract the .zip file, you may need to start WinZip in administrator mode. Do this by navigating to WinZip.exe or the WinZip Start menu entry, right-clicking on it, and selecting Run as administrator.From within WinZip, find and open the downloaded .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.

Summary: YTick labels for PLOTYY with semilog axes are incorrect in versions later than R2008a.
Description: Execute the following in or after MATLAB 7.6 (R2008a):  x = 1:10;y1 = x;y2 = [31 35.*ones(1,9)];[AX, H1, H2] = plotyy(x, y1, x, y2, 'plot', 'semilogy');set(AX(2), 'YLim', [0.01 35]);set(AX(2), 'YTick', [0.01 10 35]); Notice that the YTick  labels on the right are incorrect.
Workaround: Set the axis YScale to be of type 'log' after the call to PLOTYY:x = 1:10;y1 = x;y2 = [31 35.*ones(1,9)];[AX, H1, H2] = plotyy(x, y1, x, y2, 'plot');set(AX(2), 'YLim', [0.01 35]);set(AX(2), 'YTick', [0.01 10 35]);set(AX(2), 'YScale', 'log');

Summary: Unable to set the analog output rate on NI USB-4431 to 800 S/s
Description: The National Instruments USB-4431 supports 21 discrete sample rates from 800 S/s to 96,000 S/s. With the sample rate set to 800, an error message indicates that the SampleRate  cannot be set below the minimum value constraint. >> ao = analogoutput('nidaq','Dev1')Display Summary of Analog Output (AO) Object Using 'USB-4431'.  Output Parameters: 1250 samples per second on each channel.  Trigger Parameters: 1 'Immediate' trigger on START.  Engine status: Waiting for START.  0 total sec. of data currently queued for START.  0 samples currently queued by PUTDATA.  0 samples sent to output device since START.AO object contains no channels.>> ao.SampleRate=800??? Error using ==> daqdevice.subsasgn at 100SampleRate: Property value can not be set below the minimum value constraint. See PROPINFO(OBJ,'PROPERTY').>> propinfo(ao,'SampleRate')ans =   Type: 'double'  Constraint: 'bounded'  ConstraintValue: [1250 96000]  DefaultValue: 1250  ReadOnly: 'whileRunning'  DeviceSpecific: 0
Workaround: To work around this issue do the following:1. Quit MATLAB. 2. Make a backup copy of the following INI file:$MATLAB\toolbox\daq\daq\private\mwnidaqmx.ini where $MATLAB is the MATLAB root directory on your machine, as returned by typingmatlabrootat the MATLAB Command Prompt. Copy the file to mwnidaqmx.ini.original.3. Edit mwnidaqmx.ini and find the section for the USB-4431. 4. Change the line for the minAOSampleRate from 1250 to 800 minAOSampleRate = 800 4. Save and close mwnidaqmx.ini 5. Restart MATLAB. 6. Create the analog output object. 7. Set the sample rate of the analog output object to one of the 21 valid sample rates. 8. Start the object.IMPORTANT: If you leave the sample rate as the default, which is 1000 S/S, you will receive an error as shown below.>> ao = analogoutput('nidaq','Dev1'); % USB-4431>> addchannel(ao, 0);>> putdata(ao,zeros(1000,1))>> start(ao)??? NIDAQmx error : Measurements: Requested value is not a supported value for thisproperty. Property: DAQmx_SampClk_Rate You Have Requested: 1.0e3 You CanSelect: 800.000000, 1.250000e3, 1.500000e3, 1.600000e3, 2.500000e3,3.0e3, 3.200000e3, 5.0e3, 6.0e3, 6.400000e3, 10.0e3, 12.0e3,12.800000e3, 20.0e3, 24.0e3, 25.600000e3, 40.0e3, 48.0e3, 51.200000e3,80.0e3, 96.0e3 Task Name: MWDAT0043 Status Code: -200077Error in ==> daqdevice.start at 62start( daqgetfield(obj,'uddobject') );

Summary: Running mex -setup gives an error about $TEMP when $TEMP contains a Kanji character that has 5c as its second byte.
Description: When I run mex -setup  I get one of the following errors: C:\WORK\AKERNEL\MATLAB\BIN\MEX.PL: Error: The environment variable %TEMP% must contain the name of a writable directory.S:\BIN\MEX.PL: Error: MEX failed to create a temporary    directory.  The directory "C:\TEMP\sjis‡í±í"    defined by the environment variable %TEMP% may contain a    problematic Shift-JIS character.  Consider changing %TEMP%    to another writable directory, for example "C:\temp". This error implies %TEMP%  does not exist or is not writable. But in my case it is writable. It is possbile that this happens because %TEMP%  includes my username which has a Kanji charater in it. There is a family of Kanji characters that do not work in this case, for example Katakana So. They all have 5c  character code as the second byte of their character code. ? ?
Workaround: Change %TEMP% to point to a directory that does not contain that character. For example:C:\Temp

Summary: TriggerDelay settings with 'hwDigital' or 'hwAnalog' trigger types have no effect with National Instruments NIDAQmx devices.
Description: Setting the TriggerDelay  property on National Instruments hardware using the NIDAQmx interface has no effect.
Workaround: This is a known limitation of the toolbox, and there is no workaround available at this time.

Summary: File and folder operations can fail or names display incorrectly when names contain certain characters
Description: When file and folder names contain certain characters, the file and folder names could look incorrect and file operations could fail on Apple Macintosh and Microsoft Windows platforms. On Macintosh platforms, names must consist only of printable 7-bit ASCII characters. On Windows platforms, names must consist only of characters supported by the user locale.  If other characters appear in the name, problems such as the following might occur. In the Current Folder browser, for files and folders you created using MATLAB functions: The file or folder name does not appear correctly. This can also occur when viewing the file and folder names outside of MATLAB. Navigating to a folder fails with an error reported that the folder is nonexistant or not a directory. Trying to open a file fails, reporting a file not found error.  Functions might fail for files and folders created in the Current Folder browser or outside of MATLAB: ls  does not correctly display the file or folder name Using cd  to go to a folder with a problematic name fails with an error reported that the folder is nonexistant or not a directory Using fopen  for a problematic file fails, reporting a file not found error
Workaround: As a workaround, name files and folders using characters that work with MATLAB:On Macintosh platforms: 7-bit ASCII charactersOn Windows: characters supported by the user locale?
